narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with a solid upper-middle-class family background, you've put in the effort to build a strong profile for law school, including your undergraduate GPA of 3.50—which is actually above the typical student's 3.21—and your plan to attend full-time, just like the vast majority of applicants. However, the admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated your risk of failure at 61.9%, tipping the scales toward rejection to prioritize students with lower predicted risks. The biggest factor pulling the prediction toward failure was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 3, well below the average of 5.53, signaling to the model a notable dip in performance compared to peers at that stage. This outweighed your stronger Year 1 decile of 5, which is close to the average of 5.71 but still nudged slightly toward higher risk. Your LSAT score of 33, lower than the typical 36.70, also contributed toward the failure prediction, reinforcing concerns about exam readiness. On a brighter note, your above-average undergraduate GPA pushed meaningfully in the direction of passing, helping to soften the overall assessment. Your family income level had a minor influence toward higher risk, while your full-time status barely factored in, as it's the norm for most students. Compared to typical applicants, your profile shows strengths in GPA and commitment but stands out negatively in later-year grades and LSAT, which the model weighed heavily for bar success. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a lawyer—it's just the model's data-driven lens on bar passage patterns. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, strengthening your later academic record through additional coursework or post-bac programs, and reapplying with updated credentials that address these key areas. Many students in similar situations turn things around with targeted improvements, and we're here to support your next steps. Keep pushing—you've got the foundation to succeed.
